questions.question: What does `self.name = name` inside `__init__` do that `name = name` does not?

options.text: It stores the value on the object as an attribute, so it persists after `__init__` returns.
options.explanation: Correct. `self.name = name` attaches the value to the object itself. `name = name` just reassigns a local variable that disappears when the method exits.

options.text: It makes the attribute read-only so it can't be changed later.
options.explanation: Not quite. `self.name = name` creates a normal, writable attribute. To get read-only behavior you'd use `@property` without a setter.

options.text: It validates that `name` is a string before storing it.
options.explanation: No validation happens here. `self.name = name` stores whatever value is passed in, regardless of its type.

options.text: It shares the attribute across all instances of the class.
options.explanation: The opposite — `self.name` is per-instance. Attributes defined on the class body (outside `__init__`) are shared across all instances.

questions.question: When should you define `__repr__` instead of (or in addition to) `__str__`?

options.text: When you want a detailed, programmer-facing representation — useful in the REPL, in error messages, and when objects appear inside lists.
options.explanation: Right. `__repr__` is for debugging contexts: the interactive interpreter, collections of objects, log output. If you only define one, `__repr__` is usually the more useful choice.

options.text: When you want `print()` to display a nice human-readable message.
options.explanation: `print()` calls `__str__`, not `__repr__`. `__repr__` is used when Python needs a developer-facing description, such as inside a list or at the REPL prompt.

options.text: Only when the object contains numbers, because `__str__` can't format floats.
options.explanation: `__str__` has no trouble with floats. The choice between `__str__` and `__repr__` is about audience (end-user vs. developer), not data type.

options.text: `__repr__` is required for `sorted()` to work on your objects.
options.explanation: `sorted()` uses `__lt__`, not `__repr__`. `__repr__` controls how the object appears when printed, but has nothing to do with ordering.

questions.question: A `Square` class inherits from `Rectangle`. Its `__init__` calls `super().__init__(side, side)`. What would happen if that `super()` call were removed?

options.text: `Square` would be created without `self.width` or `self.height`, so any method that reads those attributes — like `area()` — would raise `AttributeError`.
options.explanation: Exactly. `Rectangle.__init__` is what sets `self.width` and `self.height`. Without calling `super().__init__`, those attributes are never created, and `area()` (which reads `self.width * self.height`) fails.

options.text: Python raises a `TypeError` immediately when you define the class.
options.explanation: Python doesn't check at class-definition time. The error only surfaces when you call a method that needs the missing attributes.

options.text: The `Square` would use `Rectangle`'s `__init__` automatically, because inheritance copies all methods.
options.explanation: Inheritance makes `Rectangle.__init__` available, but `Square` overrides it by defining its own `__init__`. Without the `super()` call, the parent's setup code never runs.

options.text: Nothing — `Square` doesn't need `width` and `height` because it only has `side`.
options.explanation: `Square` inherits `area()` and `perimeter()` from `Rectangle`, and both methods read `self.width` and `self.height`. Those attributes must exist on the instance.
